general-knowledge.name: General Knowledge
general-knowledge.description: Test your everyday knowledge across a wide range of topics.

questions.question: What is the capital city of Australia?
questions.explanation: Canberra is the capital city of Australia, chosen as a compromise between the rival cities of Sydney and Melbourne.

questions.question: How many sides does a hexagon have?
questions.explanation: A hexagon has 6 sides. The prefix 'hex' comes from Greek meaning six.

questions.question: What is the largest ocean on Earth?
questions.explanation: The Pacific Ocean is the largest and deepest ocean on Earth, covering more than 30% of the Earth's surface.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Romeo and Juliet'?
questions.explanation: William Shakespeare wrote Romeo and Juliet, believed to have been written between 1594 and 1596.

questions.question: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
questions.explanation: The chemical symbol for gold is Au, from the Latin word 'Aurum'.

questions.question: How many continents are there on Earth?
questions.explanation: There are 7 continents on Earth: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America.

questions.question: What is the speed of light?
questions.explanation: The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 299,792 km/s, commonly rounded to 300,000 km/s.

questions.question: Who painted the Mona Lisa?
questions.explanation: Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa, believed to have been painted between 1503 and 1519.

questions.question: What is the smallest planet in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Mercury is the smallest planet in our solar system, with a radius of only 2,439.7 km.

questions.question: What year did World War II end?
questions.explanation: World War II ended in 1945, with Germany surrendering in May and Japan surrendering in September.

entertainment.name: Entertainment
entertainment.description: Movies, music, TV shows, and pop culture trivia.

questions.question: Which movie won the first Academy Award for Best Picture?
questions.explanation: Wings (1927) won the first Academy Award for Best Picture at the inaugural ceremony in 1929.

questions.question: Who sang 'Thriller'?
questions.explanation: Michael Jackson released 'Thriller' in 1982. It remains one of the best-selling albums of all time.

questions.question: In which TV show would you find characters named Ross, Monica, and Joey?
questions.explanation: Ross Geller, Monica Geller, and Joey Tribbiani are main characters in the TV show 'Friends' (1994–2004).

questions.question: What is the highest-grossing movie of all time (not adjusted for inflation)?
questions.explanation: Avatar (2009, re-released 2022) holds the record for highest-grossing film of all time with over $2.9 billion worldwide.

questions.question: Which band was Freddie Mercury the lead singer of?
questions.explanation: Freddie Mercury was the lead vocalist of Queen, one of the most successful rock bands in history.

questions.question: What streaming platform produced 'Stranger Things'?
questions.explanation: Stranger Things is an American science fiction horror drama television series created and produced by Netflix.

questions.question: Which actor plays Iron Man in the Marvel Cinematic Universe?
questions.explanation: Robert Downey Jr. plays Tony Stark / Iron Man in the Marvel Cinematic Universe, starting with Iron Man (2008).

questions.question: What year was the first iPhone released?
questions.explanation: The first iPhone was released on June 29, 2007, announced by Steve Jobs at the Macworld Conference & Expo.

questions.question: Which video game franchise features the character Master Chief?
questions.explanation: Master Chief is the protagonist of the Halo franchise, developed by Bungie and later 343 Industries.

questions.question: What is the name of the fictional kingdom in Disney's 'Frozen'?
questions.explanation: The Kingdom of Arendelle is the fictional setting of Disney's Frozen (2013), inspired by Norway.

history.name: History
history.description: Journey through the pivotal events that shaped our world.

questions.question: In what year did Christopher Columbus first arrive in the Americas?
questions.explanation: Christopher Columbus arrived in the Americas on October 12, 1492, landing in the Bahamas.

questions.question: Which empire was ruled by Julius Caesar?
questions.explanation: Julius Caesar was a Roman general and statesman who played a critical role in the events that led to the demise of the Roman Republic.

questions.question: The Berlin Wall fell in which year?
questions.explanation: The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, marking the end of the Cold War division of Germany.

questions.question: Who was the first President of the United States?
questions.explanation: George Washington was the first President of the United States, serving from 1789 to 1797.

questions.question: In which city was the Titanic built?
questions.explanation: The RMS Titanic was built in Belfast, Northern Ireland, at the Harland and Wolff shipyard.

questions.question: Which ancient wonder was located in Alexandria, Egypt?
questions.explanation: The Lighthouse of Alexandria (Pharos) was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, built in the 3rd century BC.

questions.question: Who was the Egyptian pharaoh during the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza?
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built for Pharaoh Khufu (also known as Cheops) around 2560 BC.

questions.question: The French Revolution began in which year?
questions.explanation: The French Revolution began in 1789, starting with the storming of the Bastille on July 14, 1789.

questions.question: Who developed the theory of evolution by natural selection?
questions.explanation: Charles Darwin proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection in his 1859 book 'On the Origin of Species'.

questions.question: Which country was the first to grant women the right to vote?
questions.explanation: New Zealand was the first self-governing country to grant women the right to vote in 1893.

geography.name: Geography
geography.description: Explore the world's countries, capitals, and landmarks.

questions.question: What is the longest river in the world?
questions.explanation: The Nile River in Africa is generally considered the longest river in the world at approximately 6,650 km.

questions.question: Which country has the most natural lakes in the world?
questions.explanation: Canada has more lakes than any other country in the world, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

questions.question: What is the capital of Japan?
questions.explanation: Tokyo is the capital and largest city of Japan, and one of the most populous cities in the world.

questions.question: The Sahara Desert is located on which continent?
questions.explanation: The Sahara Desert is the world's largest hot desert, covering much of North Africa.

questions.question: What is the tallest mountain in the world?
questions.explanation: Mount Everest in the Himalayas is the tallest mountain in the world, standing at 8,848.86 meters above sea level.

questions.question: Which country is home to the Amazon Rainforest?
questions.explanation: While the Amazon Rainforest spans several countries, approximately 60% of it is located in Brazil.

questions.question: What is the smallest country in the world?
questions.explanation: Vatican City is the smallest country in the world by both area and population, covering just 0.44 km².

questions.question: Which ocean is the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation part of?
questions.explanation: The Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) is a major ocean current system in the Atlantic Ocean.

questions.question: The Great Barrier Reef is located near which country?
questions.explanation: The Great Barrier Reef, the world's largest coral reef system, is located off the coast of Queensland, Australia.

questions.question: Which is the largest country in the world by land area?
questions.explanation: Russia is the largest country in the world by land area, covering approximately 17.1 million square kilometers.

science-nature.name: Science & Nature
science-nature.description: From atoms to ecosystems — explore the wonders of science.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has an atomic number of 6, meaning it has 6 protons in its nucleus.

questions.question: What type of animal is a Komodo dragon?
questions.explanation: The Komodo dragon is the world's largest living lizard, found on the Indonesian islands of Komodo, Rinca, Flores, and Gili Motang.

questions.question: What is the powerhouse of the cell?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are often called the 'powerhouse of the cell' because they generate most of the cell's supply of ATP (energy).

questions.question: What is the most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere, making it the most abundant gas.

questions.question: How many bones are in the adult human body?
questions.explanation: An adult human body has 206 bones. Babies are born with around 300 bones, which fuse together as they grow.

questions.question: What is the process by which plants make their own food?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to produce oxygen and energy in the form of sugar.

questions.question: What planet is known as the Red Planet?
questions.explanation: Mars is called the Red Planet because its surface is covered in iron oxide (rust), giving it a reddish appearance.

questions.question: What is the hardest natural substance on Earth?
questions.explanation: Diamond is the hardest natural substance on Earth, scoring 10 on the Mohs scale of mineral hardness.

questions.question: What is the study of earthquakes called?
questions.explanation: Seismology is the scientific study of earthquakes and the propagation of elastic waves through the Earth.

questions.question: What is the primary source of energy for the water cycle?
questions.explanation: The Sun is the primary source of energy for the water cycle, driving evaporation and the movement of water through the atmosphere.

food-drink.name: Food & Drink
food-drink.description: Delicious questions about cuisines, ingredients, and culinary culture.

questions.question: Which country is the origin of sushi?
questions.explanation: Sushi originated in Japan, evolving from a Southeast Asian dish where fish was fermented with rice. Modern nigiri sushi was developed in Edo (Tokyo) in the 19th century.

questions.question: What is the main ingredient in guacamole?
questions.explanation: The main ingredient in guacamole is avocado. The word 'guacamole' comes from the Nahuatl word 'āhuacamōlli' meaning avocado sauce.

questions.question: Which cheese is traditionally used in a Greek salad?
questions.explanation: Feta cheese is the traditional Greek cheese used in Greek salad (horiatiki). It is a Protected Designation of Origin (PDO) product.

questions.question: What is sake made from?
questions.explanation: Sake is a Japanese alcoholic beverage made by fermenting rice that has been polished to remove its bran.

questions.question: Which spice gives turmeric its yellow color?
questions.explanation: Curcumin is the main chemical compound in turmeric that gives it its distinctive yellow color and many of its health benefits.

questions.question: What country invented croissants?
questions.explanation: The croissant was invented in Austria, based on the Viennese kipferl. French bakers adapted and popularized it in the form we know today.

questions.question: What type of pasta is tube-shaped?
questions.explanation: Penne is a type of pasta shaped like a tube with diagonal cuts at the ends. Its name comes from the Italian word for 'quill' or 'pen'.

questions.question: Which nut is used to make marzipan?
questions.explanation: Marzipan is made from ground almonds, sugar, and sometimes egg whites. It is commonly used in confectionery and as a cake covering.

questions.question: What is the Scoville scale used to measure?
questions.explanation: The Scoville scale measures the pungency (spiciness) of chili peppers, as defined by the amount of capsaicin they contain.

questions.question: In which country did the croissant originate?
questions.explanation: The croissant originates from Austria, where it was based on the Viennese kipferl. It was later popularized in France.

sports.name: Sports
sports.description: From the pitch to the court — test your sports knowledge.

questions.question: How many players are on a standard soccer team on the field?
questions.explanation: A standard soccer (association football) team has 11 players on the field, including the goalkeeper.

questions.question: In which sport would you perform a 'slam dunk'?
questions.explanation: A slam dunk is a type of basketball shot that is performed when a player jumps in the air and forcefully puts the ball down through the basket.

questions.question: The 2022 FIFA World Cup was held in which country?
questions.explanation: The 2022 FIFA World Cup was held in Qatar, making it the first World Cup held in the Middle East.

questions.question: How many Grand Slam tennis tournaments are there per year?
questions.explanation: There are 4 Grand Slam tennis tournaments per year: the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, and the US Open.

questions.question: Which country has won the most FIFA World Cup titles?
questions.explanation: Brazil has won the FIFA World Cup a record 5 times, in 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, and 2002.

questions.question: How long is a marathon race?
questions.explanation: A marathon is a long-distance foot race with an official distance of 42.195 km (approximately 26.2 miles or 26 miles 385 yards).

questions.question: In golf, what term describes completing a hole in one stroke under par?
questions.explanation: A birdie in golf is a score of one under par for a hole. An eagle is two under par, and an albatross (or double eagle) is three under par.

questions.question: What sport uses a puck?
questions.explanation: Ice hockey uses a puck, which is a hard vulcanized rubber disc that players hit with their sticks to score goals.

questions.question: How many players are on each side in a volleyball game?
questions.explanation: In standard volleyball, each team has 6 players on the court at a time.

questions.question: At which Olympic Games were women first allowed to compete?
questions.explanation: Women were first allowed to compete at the 1900 Paris Olympics, though they were limited to a small number of events including tennis and golf.

technology.name: Technology
technology.description: Gadgets, software, the internet, and everything tech.

questions.question: Who co-founded Apple Inc. with Steve Wozniak?
questions.explanation: Steve Jobs co-founded Apple Inc. in 1976 along with Steve Wozniak and Ronald Wayne.

questions.question: What does 'HTTP' stand for?
questions.explanation: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol, the foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web.

questions.question: Which programming language was created by Guido van Rossum?
questions.explanation: Python was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991. He designed it to emphasize code readability.

questions.question: What does 'CPU' stand for?
questions.explanation: CPU stands for Central Processing Unit, the primary component of a computer that performs most of the processing.

questions.question: In what year was the World Wide Web invented?
questions.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989 while working at CERN. He published a proposal in March 1989.

questions.question: What is the binary representation of the decimal number 10?
questions.explanation: The decimal number 10 is represented as 1010 in binary (8 + 2 = 10).

questions.question: Which company developed the Android operating system?
questions.explanation: Android was developed by Android Inc., which was acquired by Google in 2005. Google continues to develop and maintain the operating system.

questions.question: What does 'AI' stand for in technology?
questions.explanation: AI stands for Artificial Intelligence, referring to the simulation of human intelligence processes by computer systems.

questions.question: Which technology standard is used for wireless internet connections?
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi is the technology standard used for wireless internet connections, based on the IEEE 802.11 family of standards.

questions.question: What was the first commercially successful video game?
questions.explanation: Pong (1972) by Atari is considered the first commercially successful video game, though 'Computer Space' predated it.
